# c-mod99ris-572b67f97ce0 [live]
> Design the Reckoner — petition scheduler that accepts petitions from registered petitioners (vision-keepers, tech-debt watchers, laboratory/introspection, etc.), weighs them against guild priorities and resource constraints (concurrency, token budget), and determines which petitions get dispatched as writs
Status: live
Supersedes: c-mo1mqgqv "Design first-class internal commission flow (Petition)"
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:18.581Z

## c-moa42rxh-d965072428ba [parked]
> Design the vision-keeper role — the agent/apparatus that holds a forward-looking product vision and decomposes it into commissionable work
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-22T13:51:35.909Z

### c-mod53o6h-5605d3c29086 [parked]
> Introduce product and portfolio as first-class writ types with M:N codex relationships; patron owns all visions (client proxy stays private)
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:35.849Z

### c-mod53ood-521f606db4b9 [parked]
> Design the vision decomposition ladder — vision → capabilities → outcomes → commissions — with no shortcuts from vision to dispatchable work
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:36.494Z

### c-mod53p9w-4cfd83384603 [parked]
> Dogfood Nexus as the first product: author vision and initial capabilities through the new machinery
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:37.268Z

### c-mod53q2r-e7d500fa4d4a [parked]
> Should decomposition-ladder authoring be patron-only for v0, or Reckoner-proposes-patron-ratifies from day one?
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:38.307Z

### c-mod53rbz-c46fc868493d [parked]
> How is Nexus's first product-vision captured — one-shot patron-authored document or iterative elicitation ceremony?
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:39.935Z

### c-mod53rpa-34d17875d4e5 [parked]
> Plan for cross-product dependency edges in the writ data model (deferred, but model must leave room)
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:40.414Z

## c-mod53qxl-340b2ddfdf69 [parked]
> Priority storage shape: vector-at-storage with composition-at-scheduling-time, or single scalar?
Status: parked
Created: 2026-04-24T16:43:39.417Z

## c-mod9a2gh-06177cc13120 [concluded]
> Petition shape — what does a petition carry (full commission draft, brief + rationale, or intent + priority only)? Determines how much spec lives in petitioner vs. Reckoner
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Petition carries: source, intent, rationale, priority_signals (urgency + optional strategic_ref), and context_anchors. No pre-built brief or spec — specification work happens after acceptance through the existing Distiller → Sage pipeline. Uniform entry point keeps the pipeline single-track, makes declines cheap, and keeps petitioners as observers/proposers rather than planners. Rejected full-commission-draft (Option 1: duplicates Distiller across petitioner classes, wastes planning tokens on declined petitions) and brief+rationale (Option 2: same wasted-work problem, partial duplication of Distiller). Dropped cost_hint field from v0 (pushes brittle estimation onto petitioners; Reckoner can infer cost from intent + context_anchors + history instead). Lifecycle: pending → accepted | deferred | declined. Exact metadata shape carried by a deferred petition (time-based hold, condition-based resubmit, retry counters, etc.) left undefined — tracked in follow-up click c-modaqd4q.
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:32.753Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T19:21:21.985Z

## c-mod9a48y-42506a55183e [concluded]
> Patron fast-path — does a patron-posted commission flow through the petition layer (as max-priority petition) or bypass straight to writ? Tradeoff: unified flow vs. direct-write latency
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron has two first-class petition modes: imperative (priority=immediate, auto-accept, writ created immediately) and discretionary (priority in {urgent, normal, low}, goes through Reckoner weighing alongside internal petitions). Direct writ creation tools (CLI, Oculus) stay unchanged as an always-available escape hatch for edge cases (Reckoner misbehaving, recovery, debugging). Petitions carry priority as a four-level enum: immediate | urgent | normal | low. priority=immediate is authority-gated to source=patron via intake validation; internal petitioners submitting immediate are rejected at intake. Rejected full-weighing of patron petitions (inverts master/scheduler authority). Rejected pure-bypass (splits artifact space, breaks audit trail, contradicts the vocab's 'same artifact, source field' framing). Rejected auto_accept-boolean + separate priority (redundant signal — immediate IS the top priority value; one enum covers both authority-bypass and weighing). Writ-level and rig-level priority propagation intentionally not decided here — c-mod53qxl (writ priority vector-vs-scalar) and c-mocdmjk1 (rig priority, Bounty/Levy) remain live for their respective concerns.
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:35.074Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T19:40:04.095Z

## c-mod9a54n-4eb2b57168bc [live]
> Scheduling trigger — does the Reckoner evaluate petitions on a timer, on petition arrival, on writ completion, or some hybrid? Affects responsiveness vs. batching
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:36.215Z

## c-mod9a6x3-eeb50d0734cb [concluded]
> Decline feedback loop — how does a petitioner learn that its petition was declined or deferred (Lattice pulse, petition-state callback, declined-with-reason), and can it re-emit or adjust?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Two CDC-attached channels for petitioner feedback: (1) petition state transitions on the petition record itself (stacks CDC emits events on new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}, plus deferred → pending wake-ups); (2) Reckonings — append-only evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event whether or not it produced a state transition. Petitioners opt into their preferred signal model: event-driven (CDC standing-order on events matching their source), polling (query the book directly), or fire-and-forget (no subscription; re-emit when conditions change). Reckoner writes structured reason fields on each transition: on decline, decline_reason (enum: malformed | duplicate | policy_violation | source_banned | other) + optional remediation_hint (e.g., reference to an existing duplicate petition); on defer, deferral metadata per c-modaqnpt; on accept, writ_id and accepted_at. No direct callback mechanism in v0 — no mandatory subscription, all signals flow through the book via CDC. Reckoner's decline surface intentionally narrow: petition validity only (malformed, duplicate, policy violation, source banned). 'This work isn't worth doing on merit' is not a Reckoner decision — that belongs to the patron; starving-by-repeated-defer is the natural answer for work that keeps losing to competing petitions. Reckonings book design tracked in c-modc7m16.
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:38.535Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T20:02:50.730Z

## c-mod9a8fx-d468d5151982 [live]
> Petitioner registration extension point — how do new petitioner classes register with the Reckoner, and what contract must they implement (emit-petition interface, feedback receipt, lifecycle hooks)?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:40.509Z

## c-mod9a9un-141bb037d1e0 [live]
> Multi-product / plural-petitioner priority allocation — how does the Reckoner weigh petitions across multiple vision-keepers (one per product) and other petitioner classes? Shared priority space or patron-allocated budget per product?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:42.335Z

## c-mod9ab0i-8099a64462d1 [live]
> Petition dedup and conflict — how does the Reckoner handle two petitioners emitting overlapping work (merge, pick-one, let both through)? Likely deferred but worth tracking
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-24T18:40:43.842Z

## c-modaqnpt-4e4acf740487 [concluded]
> Deferred-petition metadata shape — what attributes does a deferred petition carry to support richer resubmission logic than 'consider again next cycle' (time-based hold, condition-based resubmit, retry counters, cooldown windows, escalating priority, etc.)?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Deferred petition carries: defer_reason (enum: priority | queue_depth | time_hold | patron_policy | other), defer_until (optional timestamp, time-based wake-up), defer_signal (optional event pattern, v0 reserved for future event-based wake-up), defer_count (integer), first_deferred_at, last_deferred_at, defer_note (optional freeform prose). Defer reasons narrowed to product-level Reckoner concerns only — resource_cap, budget, dependency, and rate_limit are handled by Spider/Coinmaster/Animator at the writ level after a petition is accepted, not at petition consideration. queue_depth reserved for strategic hold (Reckoner preserving prioritization authority against a Spider that might be weakly priority-aware); rare in practice if Spider becomes priority-aware (c-mocdmjk1). Wake-up logic on each Reckoner tick: check defer_until past, defer_signal matched, or triggered by writ-completion events. Escalation policies (auto-decline after N defers, nudge priority on repeat defer) are Reckoner config, not petition-shape. Petition lifecycle expanded: new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}; deferred → pending via wake-up; withdrawn is terminal and petitioner-initiated. Terminal: accepted, declined, withdrawn. Non-terminal: new, pending, deferred. Design of the Reckonings evaluation log tracked in c-modc7m16.
Created: 2026-04-24T19:21:26.417Z
Resolved: 2026-04-24T20:02:44.766Z

## c-modc7m16-20c5654ce99b [live]
> Design the Reckonings book — petition evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event (decision, reason, weighed priority, notes). Schema, retention, query patterns, CDC attachment, relationship to petition state (journal vs. materialized view)
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-24T20:02:37.002Z

content.text:
Got the context swept up. Here's where I land on the question.

## Framing

Given what we already concluded on the sibling clicks, the "petitioner contract" has shrunk a lot from what the vocab note currently implies:

- **Feedback is CDC-based** (c-mod9a6x3) — petitioners opt into standing orders on the petitions book. No `receive-feedback` interface exists; there's nothing the Reckoner *calls*.
- **Petition shape is uniform** (c-mod9a2gh) — one schema, one intake path.
- **Authority is source-keyed** (c-mod9a48y) — `priority=immediate` is gated to `source=patron`.

So what's actually left for "registration" is essentially two things:

1. **A source registry** — declarations of what sources exist and what authority each one has.
2. **Runtime lifecycle** — when/how a petitioner class *runs* to emit petitions. But this looks like a general Clockworks/standing-orders/engine concern, not a Reckoner-specific thing.

That reframes the question: **is "petitioner registration" a dedicated extension point, or is it (a) a source-policy registry + (b) existing runtime mechanisms?**

## Three candidate models

**Model A — Source-policy registry only ("petitioner is a role, not an apparatus")**

- New book: `petitioner-roster`. Rows: `source_id`, `display_name`, `allowed_priorities`, policy flags, owner/kit metadata.
- At petition intake, Reckoner (or an intake validator) looks up `source → policy`. Unknown source → rejected or flagged. Authority mismatch → rejected.
- Petitioners don't "register with the Reckoner." They register with the roster. The roster is the thing everyone (Reckoner, CLI, Oculus, auditors) consults.
- Runtime lifecycle (when to emit) is 100% the petitioner class's problem, implemented via whatever existing mechanism fits — standing orders, scheduled engines, a long-running apparatus, `nsg petition` for the patron.
- Feedback is already handled by CDC.

Pros: minimal new machinery, honest to the CDC-only feedback decision, and it matches the vocab's own line — *"Petitioner is a role contract, not an apparatus."*
Cons: the "contract" is thin. Not much for a petitioner class to implement beyond "know your source_id and emit a well-shaped petition."

**Model B — Roster + petitioner declarations in kit manifests**

Extends A. Kit/plugin manifests can declare petitioners:

```yaml
petitioners:
  - source_id: vision-keeper
    display_name: "Vision Keeper"
    runtime: standing_order   # or scheduled | interactive | external
    allowed_priorities: [urgent, normal, low]
    context_anchor_namespaces: [product, outcome]
```

Enabling a kit auto-registers its petitioners in the roster; disabling deregisters. Runtime-shape metadata enables Oculus visibility and "list me the petitioners" queries.

Pros: cleaner when the kit/plugin shape matures. Supports graceful uninstall. Machine-inspectable "who are my petitioners."
Cons: more machinery. Only pays off once kits are the normal distribution unit for new apparatus.

**Model C — Full runtime contract (petitioners-as-objects)**

Petitioners are instantiated, held by a registry, with methods the Reckoner calls. This contradicts the CDC-based feedback decision and is a regression. Reject.

## Recommendation

Go with **Model A for v0**, with a deliberate note that Model B is the natural evolution once kits/plugins are the right abstraction.

Rationale:
- Keeps the shape honest — feedback is already CDC, so there's no callback contract to build.
- Reuses existing runtime mechanisms instead of inventing a new dispatch layer.
- The roster does the real work: declares the source namespace, authority policy, and owner attribution. Everything else flows from there.
- When kits/plugins solidify, layering Model B on top is additive — the roster stays, kits just become a way to populate it.

## Open sub-questions under Model A

A few things to nail down if we go this way:

1. **Where does the roster live?** A stacks-db book (queryable, CDC-emitting), a static config file per guild, or both (file → book on boot)?
2. **Unknown-source policy** — reject hard, accept-and-flag, or configurable per guild?
3. **Roster mutability** — patron-only edits, or can kits/engines write to it? (Ties to Model B.)
4. **Intake validator location** — is it the Reckoner's responsibility, or a separate intake gate that runs *before* the Reckoner sees the petition?
5. **What does the vocab note need to be updated to?** The current line says "Petitioners register through the Reckoner's extension point and implement a contract covering: emit-petition, receive-feedback, lifecycle hooks" — that's stale given the CDC decision. If we lock Model A, that language needs rewording.

Want me to draft a fuller design note in `.scratch/` for review, or do you want to react to the framing first?

content.text:
The short answer: **there is no direct petitioner→Reckoner transport**. Petitions flow through the **petitions book**. Kit registration doesn't create a new pipe — it populates the roster and hangs the petitioner's runtime off existing kit-contribution slots.

## The actual code path

```
┌─────────────────┐      1. insert       ┌───────────────┐
│  Petitioner     │ ───────────────────► │ petitions     │
│  runtime        │                      │ book (stacks) │
│ (SO handler /   │                      └───────┬───────┘
│  engine / etc.) │                              │
└─────────────────┘                              │ 2. CDC event
                                                 │    (petition.new)
                                                 ▼
                                          ┌──────────────┐
                                          │ Reckoner     │
                                          │ standing     │
                                          │ order        │
                                          └──────┬───────┘
                                                 │ 3. evaluate,
                                                 │    update state,
                                                 │    write reckoning
                                                 ▼
                                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                                    │ petition state + reckon- │
                                    │ ings book (both CDC)     │
                                    └──────────┬───────────────┘
                                               │ 4. CDC event
                                               │    (petition.state.changed)
                                               ▼
                                       anyone who subscribed
                                       (petitioner included,
                                        via standing order)
```

Everything is book writes and CDC. No direct function call from petitioner to Reckoner ever exists.

## What kit registration actually contributes

Given that, a kit's `petitioners:` entry is really just **two things glued together**:

### (a) Roster entry — policy/identity declaration

Populates the `petitioner-roster` book on kit enable:

```yaml
petitioners:
  - source_id: vision-keeper
    display_name: "Vision Keeper"
    allowed_priorities: [urgent, normal, low]
    owner_kit: vision-keeper-kit
```

This is pure metadata. The Reckoner/intake validator reads the roster at petition intake to enforce authority.

### (b) Runtime contribution — reusing existing kit slots

The "runtime shape" field in my earlier Model B sketch isn't a new thing the kit system has to support — it's just saying *which existing contribution slot this petitioner occupies*:

```yaml
# In the same kit manifest:
standing_orders:
  - on: vision.outcome.ripe
    handler: summons:emit-vision-petition
    source_identity: vision-keeper   # ← binds the client to this source_id

# or:
engines:
  - id: tech-debt-scanner
    schedule: "0 2 * * *"            # cron
    source_identity: tech-debt-watcher
```

The kit is *already* contributing a standing order or a scheduled engine or an apparatus through the normal kit contribution points. The petitioner declaration just says "when *this* thing runs, it's running *as* source_id=X."

### (c) The emit call itself

When the petitioner runtime fires, it calls a thin typed client:

```ts
// Exposed by whatever package owns the petition layer
await client.submitPetition({
  intent: "...",
  rationale: "...",
  priority_signals: { urgency: "normal" },
  context_anchors: [...],
});
// source_id is NOT a parameter — it's bound at client construction
// from the source_identity declared in the kit manifest.
```

The client is a wrapper around `books.petitions.insert(...)` that:
- Stamps `source_id` from the bound identity (petitioner can't forge a different source)
- Stamps `submitted_at`, `submitted_by_session`
- Validates schema
- Validates authority against the roster (defense in depth — intake validator also checks)
- Returns the `petition_id`

That's the whole transport. The Reckoner never even sees the petitioner directly.

## What this clarifies about A vs. B

Given that the transport is the book, not a direct call, the difference between Model A and Model B is *not* about plumbing — it's about **who populates the roster and how**:

- **Model A**: roster is edited by hand or via a CLI. Petitioners are just things that happen to write to the book with a known source_id.
- **Model B**: roster is populated declaratively by kit manifests at install time. Petitioners are things that happen to write to the book, *plus* their source identity is bound to a kit-declared runtime so they can't forge a different source.

The real value of Model B isn't a new transport — it's **source-identity binding at the kit contribution layer**, so the client is automatically scoped and authority can't be spoofed from within a handler.

Does that match how you were thinking about it, or does the answer change your read on A vs. B?

content.text:
Fair hit — I conflated **class** and **instance**. The vision-keeper kit should contribute a *class* of petitioner, but the things that actually emit petitions are *instances*: one per product. Let me re-model.

## Class vs. instance

**Class** = archetype, contributed by a kit. One per kit. Declares:
- class name (`vision-keeper`)
- runtime template (standing order, engine, apparatus)
- whether it's product-scoped (`product_scoped: true`) or guild-wide (`product_scoped: false`)
- default authority envelope (max allowed priority, etc.)

**Instance** = a materialized petitioner tied to a specific scope. For product-scoped classes, one instance per product. Each instance gets its own roster entry and its own `source_id`.

## Roster schema (revised)

```
petitioner-roster
─────────────────
source_id              "vision-keeper:p-mods0f3j-nexus"      ← unique, per-instance
class_id               "vision-keeper"                        ← ties back to the kit class
scope                  { kind: product, product_id: p-mods0f3j-nexus }
                       # or { kind: guild } for guild-wide classes
display_name           "Nexus Vision-Keeper"
allowed_priorities     [urgent, normal, low]
owner_kit              "vision-keeper-kit"
created_at             ...
```

`source_id` is opaque from the Reckoner's perspective (just a string), but structured on write so the roster can carry the `scope` binding. The Reckoner reads `scope.product_id` on every petition to do product-aware weighing — that's the hook the multi-product priority-allocation click (c-mod9a9un) needs.

## Kit manifest (revised)

The kit contributes **one class template**, not one instance:

```yaml
petitioner_classes:
  - class_id: vision-keeper
    display_name_template: "{product.name} Vision-Keeper"
    product_scoped: true
    allowed_priorities: [urgent, normal, low]
    runtime:
      kind: standing_order
      on: vision.outcome.ripe
      handler: summons:emit-vision-petition
```

The runtime contribution is **one handler**, not N. When `vision.outcome.ripe` fires for product-X's ladder, the handler's client resolves the source identity from the event's product context (or from the roster query) and emits under `source_id=vision-keeper:product-x`. One handler, N instances, iterated by event payload.

For guild-wide classes (say `tech-debt-watcher` if it's cross-product, or `laboratory-introspection`), `product_scoped: false` and there's just one instance with `scope: { kind: guild }`.

## Instantiation

Class ≠ instance means something has to create instances. A product-scoped class gets an instance per product, so:

- **On product writ creation** — a reconciler (or a standing order bound to `product.new`) auto-materializes roster entries for every product-scoped class whose kit is enabled.
- **On kit enable** — a bootstrap sweep materializes instances for every existing product.
- **On product retirement** — instances marked dormant or dropped.

This is mechanical; no patron involvement needed beyond creating the product writ in the first place. Patron can still manually edit authority envelopes per-instance if they want (e.g., "Nexus vision-keeper is allowed urgent; the Side-Project vision-keeper is capped at normal").

## What this recovers

Now the Reckoner can genuinely see:

- Petition P from `source_id=vision-keeper:product-foo`
- Roster row says `scope.product_id = product-foo`
- Product writ says `priority = high`
- Composite weight = f(petition.priority_signals, product.priority, ...)

"High-priority feature from low-priority product" vs. "low-priority feature from high-priority product" becomes a legitimate, computable question because the product binding is **first-class on the source, not on the petition itself**. Petitioners don't have to remember to stamp product_id; it's intrinsic to their identity.

## What stays in other clicks

- **The weighing function itself** — how product priority composes with petition priority — is c-mod9a9un territory. This click just guarantees the roster carries the binding Reckoner needs.
- **Product as a writ type / priority as a field on product** — c-mod53o6h territory.

Does that restore the distinction you were flagging, or is there a further wrinkle I'm still missing?

content.text:
Sanity check done. Subtree is all **parked** — no conclusions yet, just titled placeholder clicks. So I'm comparing our discussion against the click titles + the vocab note. Here's what I find:

## Aligned

- **"One vision-keeper per product"** (vocab note) — matches our class/instance distinction directly.
- **`c-mod53o6h`: product as first-class writ type** — we need this for the roster's `scope.product_id` to reference a real entity. Our model assumes it.
- **`c-mod53ood`: ladder (vision → capabilities → outcomes → commissions)** — this gives vision-keepers something concrete to *watch*, which is what the standing-order runtime is reacting to.
- **Vocab: "standing process watching ladder state"** — matches our runtime model (standing order on writ state transitions in the ladder).

## Mismatches / things our model muddies or hasn't thought through

### 1. Portfolio is missing from our scope model

`c-mod53o6h` is titled **"product *and portfolio* as first-class writ types with M:N codex relationships"**. I quietly dropped portfolio.

If portfolios are a real layer (a grouping above product, or maybe a cross-product strategic container) and they carry priority, then `scope` in the roster isn't just `{kind: product | guild}`. It's potentially `{kind: portfolio | product | guild}`, and the priority composition gets another tier:

```
portfolio.priority × product.priority × petition.priority
```

Or portfolio might just be a grouping for rollup/visibility and *not* carry priority — in which case it doesn't affect registration. Need to ask Sean whether portfolio is a priority-bearing scope or just an organizational tag.

### 2. The trigger model is undefined

Our kit example assumed `on: vision.outcome.ripe` as a CDC event. But who marks an outcome "ripe"? Plausible answers:

- **The ladder itself** — outcome becomes ripe when its prerequisite outcomes complete (writ state CDC → ladder reconciler updates ripeness).
- **A scheduled sweep** — vision-keeper polls the ladder periodically.
- **A patron action** — patron explicitly marks "this outcome is the next thing."
- **An LLM agent** — the vision-keeper itself reasons about what's ripe (this collides with `c-mod53q2r`).

The choice changes whether the standing-order/scheduled-engine/apparatus runtime model is even correct. **`c-mod53q2r` (patron-only authoring vs Reckoner-proposes-patron-ratifies) is the key fork** and it's parked.

### 3. "Watcher" vs. "Reasoner" — the runtime shape depends on which

If vision-keeper is a *watcher* (reads ladder state, emits petitions when prerequisites met), it's a deterministic standing order — small, cheap, no LLM call needed.

If vision-keeper is a *reasoner* (looks at vision + current state and proposes the next outcome), it's an engine — LLM-backed, expensive, needs context budget.

Our kit manifest sketch (`runtime: { kind: standing_order, ... }`) only fits the first. The second would need `runtime: { kind: scheduled_engine, ... }` or `runtime: { kind: triggered_engine, ... }`. The petitioner-registration design needs to accommodate both runtime shapes — which it does in principle, but I haven't been explicit about it.

### 4. "Patron owns all visions (client proxy stays private)"

`c-mod53o6h` includes this phrase. I'm not sure what "client proxy stays private" means in context — possibly that there's a patron-side proxy/representative concept that doesn't show up inside the guild. Doesn't affect registration mechanics directly, but worth flagging — if it implies a layer between patron and product writs, our roster's `scope.product_id` reference might need to be more careful about who can edit it.

### 5. Cross-product dependencies (deferred but relevant later)

`c-mod53rpa` says: leave room in the data model for cross-product dependency edges. Implication for petitioner model: a future vision-keeper might emit a petition that names *two* products (e.g., "do work in product A that unblocks product B"). Our `scope.product_id` is single-valued. Probably fine for v0, but worth a comment in the model that scope might generalize to a list later.

## What this means for the click we're working

The petitioner-registration design holds up against the vision-keeper subtree, but it has **two real dependencies on parked clicks**:

| Dependency | Click | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Product (and possibly portfolio) as writ types | `c-mod53o6h` | Roster needs real entities to reference in `scope` |
| Authoring model (patron-only vs Reckoner-proposes) | `c-mod53q2r` | Determines runtime shape (watcher vs reasoner) |

Plus one **modeling fix** I should make right now: add `runtime.kind` enumeration to the kit manifest sketch so it explicitly covers both watcher and reasoner shapes. That's a cheap addition and keeps registration honest about what petitioners can be.

Question for you: do you want to **unpark and tackle** `c-mod53o6h` and `c-mod53q2r` before going further on registration, or **note the dependencies and proceed** with the registration design under the assumption that both will land in a compatible shape? I can argue either way — proceeding is fine if you're confident on the rough shape of those two; unparking is cleaner if you'd rather settle them before locking in registration.

Done:

- `c-moa42rxh` (vision-keeper parent) → **live**
- `c-mod53o6h` (product as writ type) → **live**, goal amended to drop portfolio
- `c-mod53q2r` (authoring model fork) → **live**
- `c-mod53rpa` (cross-product dependencies) → **left parked** — it's deliberately a "leave room in the model" reminder, not a gate
- `c-mod53p9w`, `c-mod53rbz` — **left parked** (action item + first-product elicitation; not model-shape blockers)

## Where to go next

The two newly-live clicks both feed directly into registration. Suggested order:

**1. `c-mod53o6h` — Product as writ type.** This is the lightest lift and the most foundational. We need to settle:
- Fields on a product writ (name, description, priority, lifecycle?)
- Relationship to vision (is vision a separate writ, or a field/attachment on product?)
- Ladder edges to capabilities/outcomes (writ parent/child? typed links?)
- Who can create/edit product writs (patron-only, I presume)

**2. `c-mod53q2r` — Authoring model (patron-only vs Reckoner-proposes-patron-ratifies).** This is the watcher-vs-reasoner fork that determines vision-keeper runtime shape. My lean: **patron-only for v0**. Rationale:
- Decomposition is high-leverage, low-frequency work — LLM-proposing buys little and adds a whole validation loop.
- Ladder is small (a few products, a few dozen outcomes). Manual authoring is tractable.
- Keeps vision-keeper v0 as a *watcher* (deterministic standing order on ladder state) — cheap, reliable, no LLM tokens.
- Leaves "Reckoner-proposes" as a clean future upgrade once we have signal on whether it's needed.

That lean also simplifies registration: kit's runtime contribution is `kind: standing_order`, not `kind: scheduled_engine`. Simpler v0.

Want me to dig into `c-mod53o6h` first (product writ shape) or tackle `c-mod53q2r` (authoring model) first? Or take them together since they're intertwined?

1	/**
2	 * Clerk public types.
3	 *
4	 * All types exported from @shardworks/clerk-apparatus.
5	 */
6	
7	// ── Writ phase ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8	
9	/**
10	 * A writ's position in its lifecycle.
11	 *
12	 * Transitions:
13	 *   new    → open       (publish)   — draft enters the queue
14	 *   new    → cancelled  (cancel)
15	 *   open   → completed  (complete)
16	 *   open   → failed     (fail)
17	 *   open   → cancelled  (cancel)
18	 *   open   → stuck      (engine failure cascade)
19	 *   stuck  → open       (recovery/retry resumes execution)
20	 *   stuck  → failed     (obligation abandoned)
21	 *   stuck  → cancelled  (obligation withdrawn)
22	 *
23	 * completed, failed, cancelled are terminal — no further transitions.
24	 * stuck is non-terminal — it represents a "needs attention" state.
25	 */
26	export type WritPhase = 'new' | 'open' | 'stuck' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled';
27	
28	// ── Documents ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
29	
30	/**
31	 * A writ document as stored in The Stacks.
32	 */
33	export interface WritDoc {
34	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
35	  [key: string]: unknown;
36	  /** Unique writ id (`w-{base36_timestamp}-{hex_random}`). Sortable by creation time. */
37	  id: string;
38	  /** Writ type — must be a type declared in guild config, or a built-in type. */
39	  type: string;
40	  /** Current lifecycle phase (Clerk-owned; spec side of the spec/status split). */
41	  phase: WritPhase;
42	  /**
43	   * Plugin-owned observation slot (status side of the spec/status split).
44	   *
45	   * The observation slot is a plugin-keyed map (`Record<PluginId,
46	   * unknown>`): each top-level key is a plugin id, and the value is an
47	   * arbitrary shape that plugin publishes for post-hoc observation.
48	   * Ownership is convention-only — plugin `X` writes only to `status[X]`.
49	   *
50	   * There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path:
51	   * `ClerkApi.setWritStatus(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a
52	   * transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId`
53	   * so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
54	   * `transition()` silently strips `status` from its body, and the
55	   * generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not
56	   * supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than
57	   * `setWritStatus()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber
58	   * sibling sub-slots. Readers access `writ.status?.[pluginId]` directly.
59	   *
60	   * The slot survives terminal phase transitions.
61	   */
62	  status?: Record<string, unknown>;
63	  /** Short human-readable title. */
64	  title: string;
65	  /** Detail text. */
66	  body: string;
67	  /** Target codex name. */
68	  codex?: string;
69	  /** Parent writ id. Absent on root writs. Immutable after creation. */
70	  parentId?: string;
71	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ was created. */
72	  createdAt: string;
73	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
74	  updatedAt: string;
75	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ reached a terminal state. */
76	  resolvedAt?: string;
77	  /** Summary of how the writ resolved (set on any terminal transition). */
78	  resolution?: string;
79	}
80	
81	// ── Requests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
82	
83	/**
84	 * Request to post a new commission (create a writ).
85	 */
86	/**
87	 * Request to edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
88	 * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
89	 * All fields are optional — only provided fields are updated.
90	 */
91	export interface EditWritRequest {
92	  /** Writ id. */
93	  id: string;
94	  /** New title. */
95	  title?: string;
96	  /** New body text. */
97	  body?: string;
98	  /** New writ type. Must be a valid declared type. */
99	  type?: string;
100	  /** New target codex name. Pass empty string to clear. */
101	  codex?: string;
102	}
103	
104	export interface PostCommissionRequest {
105	  /**
106	   * Writ type. Defaults to the guild's configured defaultType, or "mandate"
107	   * if no default is configured. Must be a valid declared type.
108	   */
109	  type?: string;
110	  /** Short human-readable title describing the work. */
111	  title: string;
112	  /** Detail text. */
113	  body: string;
114	  /** Optional target codex name. */
115	  codex?: string;
116	  /**
117	   * When true, the writ is created in 'new' (draft) status instead of 'open'.
118	   * Draft writs are invisible to the Spider and must be explicitly published
119	   * (new → open) before they can be picked up for execution.
120	   * Defaults to false (writ enters the queue immediately).
121	   */
122	  draft?: boolean;
123	  /**
124	   * Create this writ as a child of the specified parent writ.
125	   * The parent must be in new or open status.
126	   */
127	  parentId?: string;
128	}
129	
130	// ── Filters ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
131	
132	/**
133	 * Filters for listing writs.
134	 */
135	export interface WritFilters {
136	  /** Filter by phase. Accepts a single phase or an array of phases (OR). */
137	  phase?: WritPhase | WritPhase[];
138	  /** Filter by writ type. Accepts a single type or an array of types (OR). */
139	  type?: string | string[];
140	  /** Filter to children of this parent writ. */
141	  parentId?: string;
142	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
143	  limit?: number;
144	  /** Number of results to skip. */
145	  offset?: number;
146	}
147	
148	// ── Tree shapes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
149	
150	/**
151	 * A nested writ-and-children tree node, returned by `ClerkApi.tree()`.
152	 *
153	 * The shape is the writ document plus a recursively-typed `children` array
154	 * of further `WritTree` nodes. Mirrors ratchet's `ClickTree` so the same
155	 * mental model and rendering idioms apply across apparatus.
156	 */
157	export interface WritTree {
158	  /** The writ document at this node. */
159	  writ: WritDoc;
160	  /** Direct children, each shaped as a `WritTree`. */
161	  children: WritTree[];
162	}
163	
164	/**
165	 * Parameters for `ClerkApi.tree()`.
166	 *
167	 * - `rootId` switches between forest mode (omit) and subtree mode
168	 *   (single root). In subtree mode the returned array always has at most
169	 *   one element.
170	 * - `phase` and `type` apply prune semantics: any node failing the filter
171	 *   is dropped along with its entire subtree.
172	 * - `depth` caps recursion. The node at depth = `depth` is included but its
173	 *   children are not. `depth: 0` returns roots only.
174	 * - `rootLimit` and `rootOffset` page across the *root* layer of forest
175	 *   mode (preserves the page's `Load more` UX). They are ignored when
176	 *   `rootId` is supplied.
177	 *
178	 * In forest mode roots are returned in `createdAt desc` order (newest
179	 * first) to match `list()` and the existing writs-page UX. Children
180	 * within each subtree stay in `createdAt asc` order (oldest first) so the
181	 * visual shape of the tree is stable across sort/filter changes.
182	 */
183	export interface WritTreeParams {
184	  /** Restrict to the subtree rooted at this writ id. */
185	  rootId?: string;
186	  /** Filter by writ phase (single or OR-list); prunes non-matching subtrees. */
187	  phase?: WritPhase | WritPhase[];
188	  /** Filter by writ type (single or OR-list); prunes non-matching subtrees. */
189	  type?: string | string[];
190	  /** Maximum recursion depth (0 = roots only). Node at the cap is included. */
191	  depth?: number;
192	  /** Root-slice limit for forest mode (default: unbounded). */
193	  rootLimit?: number;
194	  /** Root-slice offset for forest mode (default: 0). */
195	  rootOffset?: number;
196	}
197	
198	// ── Configuration ───────────────────────────────────────────────
199	
200	/**
201	 * A writ type entry declared in clerk config.
202	 */
203	export interface WritTypeEntry {
204	  /** The writ type name (e.g. "mandate", "task", "bug"). */
205	  name: string;
206	  /** Optional human-readable description of this writ type. */
207	  description?: string;
208	}
209	
210	/**
211	 * Clerk apparatus configuration — lives under the `clerk` key in guild.json.
212	 */
213	export interface ClerkConfig {
214	  /** Additional writ type declarations. The built-in type "mandate" is always valid. */
215	  writTypes?: WritTypeEntry[];
216	  /** Default writ type when commission-post is called without a type (default: "mandate"). */
217	  defaultType?: string;
218	}
219	
220	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().clerk` is typed without
221	// requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
222	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
223	  interface GuildConfig {
224	    clerk?: ClerkConfig;
225	  }
226	}
227	
228	// ── Link documents ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
229	
230	/**
231	 * A link document as stored in The Stacks (clerk/links book).
232	 *
233	 * A writ link has two complementary identifiers for its relationship:
234	 *
235	 *   - `label` is a casual, human-facing string. It is open-ended, normalized
236	 *     at write time via the link-label normalization pipeline (lowercase,
237	 *     trim, camelCase split, snake_case/kebab-case split, whitespace
238	 *     collapse). Variant spellings of the same label collapse to a single
239	 *     canonical form; distinct labels remain distinct. Normalization is
240	 *     purely syntactic — it is NOT synonymy. `requires` and `depends on`
241	 *     normalize to different strings; callers that want to treat them as the
242	 *     same relationship should attach the same `kind` to both.
243	 *
244	 *   - `kind` is the load-bearing identifier. It is a stable, plugin-owned id
245	 *     drawn from the kit-contributed link-kind registry, and identifies the
246	 *     specific relationship a downstream consumer wants to react to
247	 *     (rendering parent/child, gating publish on `refines`, etc.). `null`
248	 *     when the caller treated this link as a casual label.
249	 */
250	export interface WritLinkDoc {
251	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
252	  [key: string]: unknown;
253	  /** Deterministic composite key: `{sourceId}:{targetId}:{normalized label}`. */
254	  id: string;
255	  /** The writ that is the origin of this relationship. */
256	  sourceId: string;
257	  /** The writ that is the target of this relationship. */
258	  targetId: string;
259	  /**
260	   * Casual relationship label — an open string normalized syntactically at
261	   * write time (e.g. "fixes", "retries", "supersedes", "duplicates",
262	   * "depends on"). NOT a synonymy layer. For load-bearing identity, use
263	   * `kind`.
264	   */
265	  label: string;
266	  /**
267	   * Load-bearing identifier for this relationship. When non-null, references
268	   * a registered kind id from the `linkKinds` kit registry and is the field
269	   * downstream consumers key on. Always present on each row (`null` when
270	   * the caller did not attach a kind).
271	   */
272	  kind: string | null;
273	  /** ISO timestamp when the link was created. */
274	  createdAt: string;
275	}
276	
277	// ── Link kind registry ───────────────────────────────────────────────
278	
279	/**
280	 * A link-kind contribution declared by a kit's `linkKinds` array.
281	 *
282	 * Kit authors use this shape when populating `ClerkKit.linkKinds`. Kind ids
283	 * must be prefixed with the contributing plugin's id followed by `.` and a
284	 * kebab-case suffix — e.g. kit `astrolabe` contributing `astrolabe.refines`.
285	 * The registry-projection view (returned by `listKinds()`) is `LinkKindDoc`,
286	 * which embeds the resolved owner plugin id; kit authors do not repeat their
287	 * plugin id on each entry.
288	 */
289	export interface KindEntry {
290	  /**
291	   * Fully-qualified kind id. Must begin with `{pluginId}.` and be followed
292	   * by a non-empty kebab-case suffix (lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens,
293	   * not starting or ending with a hyphen).
294	   */
295	  id: string;
296	  /** Human-readable description of the relationship this kind denotes. */
297	  description: string;
298	}
299	
300	/**
301	 * A link-kind record as projected by `listKinds()`.
302	 *
303	 * Unlike `KindEntry`, this shape embeds the resolved owner plugin id so
304	 * consumers of the registry do not need to parse it back out of `id`.
305	 */
306	export interface LinkKindDoc {
307	  /** Fully-qualified kind id. */
308	  id: string;
309	  /** Plugin id of the kit that contributed this kind. */
310	  ownerPlugin: string;
311	  /** Human-readable description of this kind. */
312	  description: string;
313	}
314	
315	/**
316	 * Result of querying links for a writ — both directions in one response.
317	 */
318	export interface WritLinks {
319	  /** Links where this writ is the source (this writ → other writ). */
320	  outbound: WritLinkDoc[];
321	  /** Links where this writ is the target (other writ → this writ). */
322	  inbound: WritLinkDoc[];
323	}
324	
325	// ── Writ type metadata ──────────────────────────────────────────────
326	
327	/**
328	 * Metadata for a registered writ type, returned by listWritTypes().
329	 */
330	export interface WritTypeInfo {
331	  /** The writ type name. */
332	  name: string;
333	  /** Human-readable description, or null if none was provided. */
334	  description: string | null;
335	  /** Origin of this type: "builtin", "guild", or the contributing plugin id. */
336	  source: string;
337	  /** Whether this is the guild's default writ type. */
338	  isDefault: boolean;
339	}
340	
341	// ── API ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
342	
343	/**
344	 * The Clerk's runtime API — retrieved via guild().apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk').
345	 */
346	export interface ClerkApi {
347	  /**
348	   * Post a new commission, creating a writ in 'open' status by default.
349	   * If `request.draft` is true, the writ is created in 'new' status instead
350	   * and will not be picked up by the Spider until explicitly published.
351	   * Validates the writ type against declared types in guild config.
352	   */
353	  post(request: PostCommissionRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
354	
355	  /**
356	   * Show a writ by id. Throws if not found.
357	   */
358	  show(id: string): Promise<WritDoc>;
359	
360	  /**
361	   * Resolve a writ id prefix to the full id. Mirrors ratchet's resolveId
362	   * for clicks — callers may pass the short display form (e.g. `w-mo2xi6pt`)
363	   * and receive the full id back. Throws when no writ matches or when the
364	   * prefix is ambiguous.
365	   */
366	  resolveId(prefix: string): Promise<string>;
367	
368	  /**
369	   * List writs with optional filters, ordered by createdAt descending.
370	   */
371	  list(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<WritDoc[]>;
372	
373	  /**
374	   * Count writs matching optional filters.
375	   */
376	  count(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<number>;
377	
378	  /**
379	   * Walk the writ hierarchy and return a forest (or a single subtree when
380	   * `rootId` is provided). Mirrors ratchet's `tree()` precedent.
381	   *
382	   * Filters apply with prune semantics: a node failing `phase` or `type`
383	   * is excluded together with its entire subtree. The `depth` cap halts
384	   * recursion below — the node at the cap is still included, but no
385	   * descendants of it are walked. `rootLimit` / `rootOffset` page across
386	   * the root layer of forest mode and are ignored when `rootId` is given.
387	   */
388	  tree(params?: WritTreeParams): Promise<WritTree[]>;
389	
390	  /**
391	   * Count all descendants of a writ grouped by phase.
392	   *
393	   * Walks the parent/child tree recursively beneath `writId`, tallying each
394	   * descendant's phase. The root writ (the writ identified by `writId`) is
395	   * excluded from the count — only its descendants contribute. The result is
396	   * a plain object keyed by `WritPhase` with numeric values; phases with no
397	   * matching descendants are simply absent.
398	   *
399	   * This is the traversal primitive behind `writ-show`'s `children.summary`
400	   * field, and is also reusable by any future subtree-oriented tooling (e.g.
401	   * a `writ-tree` apparatus). Throws if the writ does not exist.
402	   */
403	  countDescendantsByPhase(writId: string): Promise<Record<WritPhase, number>>;
404	
405	  /**
406	   * Transition a writ to a new phase, optionally setting additional fields.
407	   * Validates that the transition is legal.
408	   *
409	   * Managed fields supplied in `fields` are silently dropped before the
410	   * patch is applied: `id`, `phase`, `status`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`,
411	   * `resolvedAt`, and `parentId`. `phase` is managed by the state machine;
412	   * the plugin-owned observation slot `status` is writable only via
413	   * `setWritStatus()` (the one sanctioned slot-write path) so that
414	   * sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers. The generic
415	   * `put()` / `patch()` paths are not supported slot-write mechanisms.
416	   */
417	  transition(id: string, to: WritPhase, fields?: Partial<WritDoc>): Promise<WritDoc>;
418	
419	  /**
420	   * Write a plugin-owned sub-slot of the writ's observation `status` map.
421	   *
422	   * The slot is the "status" side of the spec/status split: plugins publish
423	   * post-hoc observations here (stuck causes, gate state, provenance, …)
424	   * without polluting the Clerk-owned `phase` state machine.
425	   *
426	   * The call is a transactional read-modify-write — the sub-slot keyed by
427	   * `pluginId` is replaced with `value`, but sibling sub-slots owned by
428	   * other plugins are preserved. Writes emit CDC update events like any

content.content:
5:> **On pluralization:** Where terms derive from Latin or other classical roots, this document uses English plurals throughout — *animas* not *animae*, *codexes* not *codices*. Accessibility over pedantry.
17:The human. The patron commissions work from the guild and consumes what it delivers. The patron interacts through the guild's interfaces — CLI, status reports, delivered works — and judges those works by using them. The patron may assign codexes as targets for commissions ("build the next thing in this codex"), but does not direct how the guild organizes its labor.
103:A body of inscriptions that compels a system to behave. The guild's primary and most complex work product. Bindings live in codexes, accumulate across successive commissions, and govern the behavior of running systems.
107:| **Binding** | The complete body of inscriptions in a codex governing a system's behavior |
115:A commission arrives; the Spider opens a draft binding from the codex; an anima staffs the engine — inscribing changes, building up the draft. When the anima signals completion, the sealing engine incorporates the draft into the sealed binding. The codex grows. If the draft contradicts the sealed binding, the sealing engine seizes; the draft must be reconciled before sealing can proceed.
117:A codex may have multiple draft bindings open simultaneously. Each is independent. Each must be sealed or abandoned on its own terms.
129:## Codexes
131:The canonical record of a body of work — assigned to the guild by the patron or maintained for its own operations. The guild works *toward* a codex across successive commissions, each one inscribing more into it.
133:Some codexes hold works for the patron — applications, services, deployed systems. Others are purely guild infrastructure, maintained for the guild's own operations.
183:The apparatus that maintains the guild's knowledge of its codexes. When a codex is registered, the Surveyor inspects it — determining what kinds of work are applicable and how each is fulfilled for that specific codex. When a codex changes, the Surveyor updates its records. The guild's ability to seed rigs from commission text depends on the Surveyor's knowledge: without a current survey, the guild cannot reliably turn a patron's words into a working rig.
185:The Surveyor's records live in the guildhall, not in the codexes themselves — a survey is the guild's understanding of a codex, not part of the codex's own inscriptions.
189:The guild's institutional center — a home, not a codex. The guildhall is where the charter hangs on the wall, where the tools are stored, where the register is kept, where training content lives. Work doesn't happen here; this is where the guild's knowledge, configuration, and equipment are maintained. Always present, always accessible.
191:Distinct from codexes: codexes are where the guild's inscriptions accumulate. The guildhall is the building they come from — the place that tells them who they are and equips them for the job.

content.text:
Good — I dug into the framework and found the relevant machinery already exists. Here's the lay of the land.

## What's already there (writ-type registration)

The Clerk already has a full kit contribution system for writ types:

```ts
// @shardworks/clerk-apparatus
export interface ClerkKit {
  writTypes?: WritTypeEntry[];   // new writ types
  linkKinds?: KindEntry[];        // new link-kind relationships
}
```

Any plugin can contribute `writTypes: [{ name: 'product', description: '...' }]` and the Clerk will accept `type: 'product'` on `post()`. The listWritTypes() API tags each type with its source (`builtin | guild | pluginId`). Built-in is just `mandate`; everything else is kit- or config-contributed.

So **the apparatus for registering new writ types already exists.** "Product as a writ type" doesn't need framework changes — it needs a plugin that declares it.

## What DOES need designing (the real gap)

The generic `WritDoc` schema is:

```
{ id, type, phase, title, body, codex?, parentId?,
  status?, createdAt, updatedAt, resolvedAt?, resolution? }
```

That's it. No typed payload per writ type. Which means: **where does product-specific data live?**

Relevant examples:
- `priority` (Reckoner needs this for weighting)
- `vision` text (if it's separate from `body`)
- `lifecycle stage` (draft / active / sunset)
- `owner` or `stakeholder`

Four options, with real trade-offs:

### Option 1 — Everything in `body`, unstructured

Put priority, vision, lifecycle as YAML/JSON blobs in the `body` field. The Reckoner parses body to read priority.

- **Pro:** Zero framework change. Works today.
- **Con:** Not queryable without full-text scans. Reckoner has to deserialize every product writ on every scheduling tick. Breaks when body format drifts. Feels wrong.

### Option 2 — Store typed data in `status[pluginId]`

Use the plugin-owned observation slot. `writ.status['vision-keeper']` holds `{ priority, vision, stage }`.

- **Pro:** No framework change. Sanctioned slot-write path (`setWritStatus`) handles concurrency.
- **Con:** "Status" semantically means *observations* — "plugin X observes this about the writ." Priority is not an observation; it's a core attribute of the writ. Using `status` for it bends the metaphor. Also queryability depends on whether stacks can filter on nested JSON paths (probably yes with sqlite JSON1, but awkward).

### Option 3 — Companion book keyed by writ_id

A separate `product-details` book owned by the vision-keeper plugin, with rows keyed by product writ id, holding `{ priority, vision, stage, ... }`. Deleted via CDC standing order when the writ is cancelled.

- **Pro:** Proper separation — Clerk owns writ lifecycle, product plugin owns product-specific data. Queryable directly. Schema-evolvable. Idiomatic plugin pattern.
- **Con:** Two-book writes (Clerk posts the writ, then plugin inserts the details row). Slightly more ceremony on create. Needs lifecycle-coupling to avoid orphans.

### Option 4 — Add typed payload to WritDoc

Extend `WritDoc` with a typed `payload` field, validated by a schema associated with the writ type.

- **Pro:** Strongest typing, single round-trip create. Clerk enforces schema.
- **Con:** Large framework change. Now every writ type needs a registered schema. Opens questions about schema evolution. Overkill for v0.

## My recommendation

**Option 3 — companion book.** Clean separation, no framework pressure, aligns with the existing "plugin owns its own books" pattern that stacks/CDC is built around. The Reckoner's standing order reads `product-details` when it needs priority; CDC keeps things in sync.

## What a "product-layer" (or "vision") plugin looks like for v0

Gathering everything: to make `c-mod53o6h` (product writ type) real, we need **a new plugin** — call it `vision` or `product-layer` — that contributes:

1. **Writ types:** `product`, `capability`, `outcome` (the ladder tiers)
2. **Link kinds:** e.g. `vision.realizes` (outcome → capability), `vision.part-of` (capability → product) — or alternatively rely on `parentId` for the hierarchy and only add link-kinds for non-tree relationships
3. **Companion books:** `product-details`, `capability-details`, `outcome-details` keyed by writ_id
4. **CLI commands:** `nsg product create`, `nsg product set-priority`, etc.
5. **(Later)** The vision-keeper petitioner class — runtime handler watching ladder state
6. **(Later)** Petitioner kit contribution (pending our registration design for `c-mod9a8fx`)

## Cascading clicks

Three real questions pop out that should probably be clicks under `c-mod53o6h`:

1. **Where do product-specific fields live?** (The Option 1-4 question above.)
2. **Parent/child vs. links for the ladder edges?** (Tree-native vs. typed-link-native.)
3. **Ladder invariants** — does the system enforce "outcome must be child of a capability" or is it convention-only?

And one cross-cutting click:

4. **Is "product-layer" one plugin or split?** — i.e., does the plugin that declares `product` writ type also own the vision-keeper petitioner runtime, or are those separate plugins that happen to compose?

Want me to:
- (a) Spin up those four sub-clicks and tackle them?
- (b) Pick one to resolve now and leave the others for later?
- (c) Go even deeper first — poke at whether there's a pattern already for "typed data on writs" elsewhere in the plugin ecosystem (astrolabe's plandocs, say) before picking Option 3?

---

## Open Questions

- **Should `commission-post` be a permissionless tool?** It represents patron authority — commissions come from outside the guild. But Coco (running inside a session) needs to call it. Current thinking: gate it with `clerk:write` and grant that to the steward role.

- **Writ type validation — strict or advisory?** The Clerk validates against `clerk.writTypes` in config. But this means adding a new type requires a config change. Alternative: accept any string, use the config list only for documentation/tooling hints. Current thinking: strict validation — the guild should know its own vocabulary.

---

## Implementation Notes

- Standalone apparatus package at `packages/plugins/clerk/`. Requires only the Stacks.
- `WritDoc.type` uses a guild-defined vocabulary, not a framework enum. The Clerk validates against `clerk.writTypes` in the apparatus config section but the framework imposes no meaning on the type name.
- Writ ids use the format `w-{base36_timestamp}-{hex_random}`, produced by `generateId('w', 6)` — sortable by creation time, unique without coordination. Not a formal ULID, but provides the same useful properties (temporal ordering, no coordination).
- The `transition()` method is the single choke point for all phase changes. All tools and future integrations go through it. This is where validation, timestamp setting, event emission, and hierarchy cascade happen. The observation slot `status` is a managed field stripped from the body alongside `id`, `phase`, timestamps, and `parentId`; the one sanctioned slot-write path is `setWritStatus()`, which performs a transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId` so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
- When the Clockworks is eventually added as a recommended dependency, resolve it at emit time via `guild().apparatus()`, not at startup — so the Clerk functions with or without it.
- Parent/child cascade uses a Phase 1 CDC watcher (`failOnError: true`) so cascade operations are transactional — if a cascade step fails, the triggering transition rolls back.
- `parentId` is immutable: stripped from managed fields in `transition()`, preventing mutation through the API.

# The Reckoner — API Contract

Status: **Draft**

Package: `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus` · Plugin id: `reckoner`

> **⚠️ MVP scope.** MVP ships three trigger types (`writ-stuck`,
> `writ-failed`, `queue-drained`), roots-only scoping, and the
> clockworks-retry soft dependency. Every other Reckoner-style trigger
> (`needs-review`, anima completion, coinmaster alerts, …) is out of scope.

---

## Purpose

The Reckoner is a narrow observer: a Phase 2 CDC watcher on `clerk/writs`
that emits Lattice pulses when a *commission* stalls, fails, or when the
guild's work queue drains. It turns the Clerk's phase-transition events into
the first three observer-emitted pulses on the Lattice substrate.

The Reckoner does **not** decide how pulses are delivered; that is the
Lattice's job. It does not modify writs, does not inspect rig internals, and
does not interact with Spider's dispatch path. It reads two books
(`clerk/writs` and `spider/rigs`) and writes one API (`LatticeApi.emit`).

The Reckoner is install-only configurable: there is no `guild.json` section
for it. Install turns it on, uninstall turns it off. Decisions about *which*
pulses get delivered live on the Lattice's channel-configuration side, not
here.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires:   ['clerk', 'lattice', 'stacks']
recommends: ['spider', 'clockworks-retry', 'oculus']
```

- **The Clerk** (required) — source of writ transitions the Reckoner
  watches.
- **The Lattice** (required) — consumer of every pulse the Reckoner emits.
- **The Stacks** (required) — CDC substrate plus the rigs book the
  Reckoner reads for cap evaluation and drain counts.
- **Spider** (recommended) — owner of the `rigs` book. When Spider is
  absent the rig counts resolve to zero, `isQueueDrained` degrades
  gracefully, and retry-cap evaluation falls back to the fail-safe
  `isTerminalStuck` path.
- **Clockworks-retry** (recommended) — source of the `maxAttempts` cap. The
  soft-dependency resolution is described below.

19:Writ documents follow a Kubernetes-style spec/status split: **`phase`** is the Clerk-owned lifecycle state (the phase machine below), and **`status`** is a plugin-owned observation slot — a `Record<string, unknown>` keyed by plugin id where apparatuses like Spider record side-channel observations (last rig, stuck cause, progress ratchets). See [Spec/Status Convention](#specstatus-convention).
227:   * stripped. The observation slot `status` is plugin-owned and
306:  /** Plugin-owned observation slot, keyed by plugin id. Side-channel
438:Writ documents follow a Kubernetes-style spec/status split:
441:- **Status slot** (`status` on `WritDoc`) is a free-form `Record<string, unknown>` keyed by plugin id. Each plugin owns one sub-slot and records side-channel observations there: last rig, stuck cause, progress ratchets, planner version, etc. `setWritStatus()` is the only writer for the slot.
446:- **One sanctioned slot-write path.** The observation slot is writable only via `setWritStatus()`, which performs a transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId` so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers. `transition()` silently drops `status` from its body alongside the other managed fields. The generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the `clerk/writs` book are not supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than `setWritStatus()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber sibling sub-slots.
480:The spec/status split is guild-wide in intent, not a writs-only pattern. Other runtime objects — **rigs**, **engines**, **sessions**, **input requests**, **clicks**, and future apparatuses' primary objects — will adopt the same split on a per-consumer basis: the owning apparatus keeps the lifecycle field (renaming its current `status` to `phase` when the time comes), and a new plugin-keyed `status: Record<string, unknown>` slot appears when the first observation-slot consumer materializes. Until that trigger arrives, those objects keep their existing `status` field unchanged — the convention rolls out one object at a time, not in a big-bang migration. Apparatus authors adding a new primary object whose state may gain observations should reach for the spec/status shape from day one to avoid the rename later.
630:- The `transition()` method is the single choke point for all phase changes. All tools and future integrations go through it. This is where validation, timestamp setting, event emission, and hierarchy cascade happen. The observation slot `status` is a managed field stripped from the body alongside `id`, `phase`, timestamps, and `parentId`; the one sanctioned slot-write path is `setWritStatus()`, which performs a transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId` so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.

  codex: string;
  status: PlanStatus;    // ← its own state machine ('reading' | 'analyzing' | ...)
  inventory?: string;
  observations?: Observation[];
  scope?: ScopeItem[];
  decisions?: Decision[];
  spec?: string;
  createdAt: string;
  updatedAt: string;
}
```

Notable: `PlanDoc.id === writ.id`. There's no separate `writId` foreign key — the plan's primary key *is* the writ id. Plugin owns the book (`plansBook: Book<PlanDoc>`), exposes a typed API (`astrolabe.show(planId)`, `.patch()`, etc.), and PlanDoc has its own state machine (`PlanStatus`) separate from `WritPhase`. This is idiomatic.

### Clerk docs are explicit about what `status` is for

From `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus/clerk.md`:

> Writ documents follow a Kubernetes-style spec/status split: `phase` is the Clerk-owned lifecycle state, and `status` is a plugin-owned observation slot — a Record<string, unknown> keyed by plugin id where apparatuses like Spider record **side-channel observations** (last rig, stuck cause, progress ratchets).

And later:

> Each plugin owns one sub-slot and records **side-channel observations** there: last rig, stuck cause, progress ratchets, planner version, etc.

Spider's use of `status.spider` matches this perfectly — stuck cause, blocker ids, observed-at timestamps. All *observations about* the writ, not *attributes of* the writ.

**This is load-bearing for our question:** the arch doc's own language ("observation slot," "side-channel observations") puts Option 2 off the table for core attributes like `priority`. Priority on a product isn't an observation; it's an intrinsic property.

### Strict writ-type validation is confirmed policy

Clerk arch doc notes an open question "strict or advisory?" and resolves it: **strict** — "the guild should know its own vocabulary." So adding a new writ type *does* require a manifest/config change. This is the contract: you can't just post a writ with a made-up type and have it accepted.

### One aside worth flagging (not for this click)

There's a **plugin naming collision** coming: `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus` already exists as a narrow MVP pulse emitter (writ-stuck, writ-failed, queue-drained — emits to the Lattice). It's structurally very different from the petition scheduler we're designing. When we implement the new Reckoner, we'll need to rename/subsume/deprecate the existing one. Worth a click somewhere under `c-mod99ris`, not under this subtree. Want me to spin one up?

---

## Lay of the land — field placement (`c-modee576`)

### The four options, re-anchored against prior art

| | Option 1: body blob | Option 2: status slot | Option 3: companion book | Option 4: typed writ payload |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Prior art** | None | Spider, others — but only for *observations* | Astrolabe PlanDoc — the main pattern | None |
| **Framework change** | No | No | No | Yes (Clerk schema) |
| **Queryable by Reckoner** | No (full-text scan + parse) | Yes (sqlite JSON paths) but awkward | Yes (direct book filter) | Yes (direct field filter) |
| **Schema evolution** | Unstructured; breaks easily | Free-form record; no validation | Plugin-owned; evolvable | Clerk-level; harder |
| **Concurrency-safe writes** | `transition()` clobbers | `setWritStatus()` RMW | Normal book writes + CDC | Via `transition()` |
| **Lifecycle coupling** | Tied to writ | Tied to writ (survives terminal) | Need explicit cascade | Tied to writ |
| **Semantic fit for core attrs** | Wrong — body is prose | Wrong — slot is for observations | Right — plugin-owned typed data | Right but overkill |

### Where the options land given the prior art

**Option 1 (body blob)** — dismissed. Nothing in the codebase does this. Body is prose. Dead.

**Option 2 (status slot)** — **ruled out by the arch doc's own semantics.** Status is explicitly for observations, not intrinsic attributes. Using it for priority would bend the metaphor *and* break the established convention. If later we want to record *observations about* a product (say, "vision-keeper last-scanned-at: T"), status.vision-keeper is appropriate for that. But not for the product's priority itself.

**Option 3 (companion book)** — **strongly supported by prior art.** Astrolabe's PlanDoc is the template. Apply the same pattern: a `products` book owned by the vision plugin, keyed by writ id, holding `{ id, priority, vision, stage, owner, createdAt, updatedAt }`. Plugin exposes a typed API. CDC handles cascade on writ termination.

**Option 4 (typed writ payload)** — overkill and not on the table anywhere. Would be a large framework change for no clear benefit when Option 3 gives us everything except single-round-trip creation. If we ever need it, we'd want to see multiple plugins hit the same limitation first.

### My recommendation

**Option 3, modeled on Astrolabe's PlanDoc.**

Concrete sketch:

```ts
// @shardworks/vision-apparatus (or similar)

export type ProductStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'sunset';
export type Priority = 'critical' | 'high' | 'normal' | 'low';

export interface ProductDoc {
  [key: string]: unknown;
  id: string;                    // === writ.id
  priority: Priority;
  stage: ProductStage;
  visionRef?: string;            // pointer to vision text (codex location, attachment id, or inline markdown in writ.body)
  owner?: string;                // stakeholder / product owner
  createdAt: string;
  updatedAt: string;
}

export interface CapabilityDoc { /* similar shape */ }
export interface OutcomeDoc { /* similar shape */ }
```

The vision plugin owns three books (`products`, `capabilities`, `outcomes`), keyed by writ id, and exposes `visionApi.getProduct(writId)`, `.setPriority()`, etc. Reckoner scheduling reads `products` directly for priority (or calls `visionApi.getProduct()` through the apparatus API).

### Two follow-on design questions this surfaces

1. **Where does vision text live?** Three options:
   - Inside `writ.body` as markdown — simple, tied to writ, edited via `clerk.edit()`.
   - In `ProductDoc.visionRef` as a codex path — versioned with the codex, collaboratively editable.
   - As a stacks attachment (if that exists; I'd need to check).
   I lean toward **writ.body for v0** because it's the simplest and vision text isn't that long. Can migrate to codex-backed later if it grows.

2. **Lifecycle coupling** — when a product writ is cancelled, what happens to its `ProductDoc`? Options:
   - Leave it (orphan; harmless but clutters the book).
   - CDC standing order deletes on terminal transition.
   - Keep but mark archived.
   I'd pick **CDC delete on cancelled; keep on completed/failed** — completed products might be historical record; cancelled means "never mind."

### What this click would conclude

If you agree with the direction: **Option 3, one book per writ type in the ladder (`products`, `capabilities`, `outcomes`), keyed by writ id; typed API on the vision plugin; vision text in `writ.body` for v0; CDC-cascade cleanup on cancellation.**

React or push back? Anything in there feel wrong given the way other plugins work?

I think we can conclude the click with one caveat -- all these names (vision, capability, outcome, product) are just placeholders. Similarly, I'm not sure I like the 'XxxDoc' convention. content.text:
## `c-modee5kb` — Ladder edges: parent/child vs. typed links

### What's already in place

The Clerk supports both models:

- **`parentId`** on `WritDoc` — optional, immutable after creation, no type restriction on parent. `clerk.tree()` walks parent/child to return a `WritTree[]` forest/subtree. `clerk.countDescendantsByPhase()` rolls up descendant state. Parent/child cascade on terminal transitions is already wired.
- **Typed links** — via `clerk.link(sourceId, targetId, label, kind?)`. Kit-contributed `linkKinds` (e.g., `astrolabe.refines`, `astrolabe.lifted-from`) give downstream consumers stable ids to key on. Bidirectional via `clerk.links(writId)`.

So both mechanisms exist; the question is **which one represents the ladder edge** (product → capability → outcome → commission).

### Three positions

**Position A — Pure parent/child**

Use `parentId` for every ladder edge. `capability.parentId = product.id`, `outcome.parentId = capability.id`, `mandate.parentId = outcome.id`.

- **Pros:** Zero new machinery. `clerk.tree({ rootId: productId })` gives you the entire ladder for a product in one call. Existing UIs that render writ trees (Oculus, CLI writ-tree) work without modification. `countDescendantsByPhase` gives free rollups. Single authoritative edge per node.
- **Cons:** Single parent only — no DAG. A capability can't belong to two products. An outcome can't realize two capabilities. (Deferred per `c-mod53rpa`, but worth naming.)

**Position B — Pure typed links**

No `parentId`. Every ladder edge is a typed link: `vision.realizes` (outcome → capability), `vision.part-of` (capability → product), `vision.fulfills` (mandate → outcome).

- **Pros:** DAG from day one. Explicit edge semantics at the data-model level. Cross-product relationships work the same way as intra-product.
- **Cons:** `clerk.tree()` becomes useless for the ladder — we'd need a new ladder-walk API. Existing writ-tree UIs don't see the structure. Every render has to follow links. No free rollups. Much more machinery for no immediate benefit (we've deferred DAG).

**Position C — Hybrid: parent/child primary, typed links auxiliary**

`parentId` carries the primary ladder edge (the one-product-per-capability, one-capability-per-outcome spine). Typed links reserved for cross-product / DAG / auxiliary relationships when they arrive.

- **Pros:** All of A's benefits for v0 plus a growth path. When `c-mod53rpa` lands, we add link kinds like `vision.also-serves` for a capability that lives primarily under product X but also serves product Y — tree structure stays intact, extra relationships layer on top.
- **Cons:** Two edge models in the eventual end-state. But: "primary hierarchy on parentId, auxiliary relationships on links" is already the prevailing pattern in the codebase (Astrolabe uses `astrolabe.lifted-from` alongside normal writ hierarchy).

### What prior art says

Astrolabe's pattern is clearly hybrid:

- Normal parent/child for the default writ hierarchy
- `astrolabe.lifted-from` link kind for provenance that doesn't fit the tree
- `astrolabe.refines` for a semantic relationship that's *not* "is a child of"

That's a direct template. The `observation-set` → observation relationship uses parentId; the observation → originating mandate relationship uses `lifted-from`. Same ecosystem, two edge models, each used for what it's good at.

### My recommendation

**Position C — hybrid, parent/child primary.**

For v0:
- `capability.parentId = product.id`
- `outcome.parentId = capability.id`
- `mandate.parentId = outcome.id` (when spawned from a petition whose context_anchor names an outcome — more on that below)
- **No** link kinds registered yet by the vision plugin — we don't have a concrete use case to motivate one in v0.

Growth path: when `c-mod53rpa` (cross-product dependencies) is ready, we add kit-contributed link kinds (`vision.also-serves`, `vision.depends-on`, whatever shape lands), and existing single-parent ladders keep working unchanged.

### One thing this surfaces for the next click

`c-modee5x4` (ladder invariants) gets a sharp framing now. If we use parentId, the natural invariants are:

- `capability.parentId → product`
- `outcome.parentId → capability`

No restriction on `mandate.parentId` (mandates are free-form and already used everywhere for all kinds of parent-child). These are enforced at *create time* — `clerk.post({ type: 'capability', parentId: X })` validates that X is a `product` writ. Either the Clerk does that directly (seems unlikely — it's type-agnostic) or the vision plugin's typed API (`createCapability()`) is the only sanctioned creation path and does the check. The latter is cleaner.

### One thing this does NOT settle

**Mandate parenting.** When the Reckoner accepts a petition whose `context_anchors` include an outcome, the resulting mandate's parent could be:
- The outcome (natural — "this commission realizes this outcome")
- Nothing (root-level mandate, linked to the outcome via a typed link)

Position C leans toward "parent = outcome" for the same reasons we picked parent/child for the ladder. But this touches the petition→writ materialization path and might be better decided under the Reckoner's own clicks. I'll flag it as a cross-reference rather than re-deciding it here.

### Proposed conclusion

If you agree: **hybrid model, parent/child for ladder edges (product ← capability ← outcome), no typed link kinds in v0, cross-product/DAG relationships via future link kinds under `c-mod53rpa`.** Note that ladder invariant enforcement (`c-modee5x4`) will build on this. Mandate parenting cross-referenced to the Reckoner subtree.

React or push back?
